channel.title: Center for Houston's Future
channel.description: Houston's premier cross-sector think tank driving research, civic leadership, and strategic initiatives for the region's future.

item.title: Future Houston
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded: Center for Houston's Future brings business, government, community and academic stakeholders together for planning, research, consensus building and action.

Exploring how innovation goes hand in hand with play, curiosity and experimentation

item.title: About the Center
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Strategic Initiatives

Where we focus.

We conduct economic research and strategic planning, hold community events and develop leaders.

All Initiatives →
Current Initiative

Project Metis

Positioning the Houston/Galveston region as the global leader in brain health and the emerging brain economy.
Learn More →
Current Initiative

Vision 2050

Creating a long-range strategic plan and vision for the Houston region with business, community, and government leaders.
Learn More →
Past Initiatives

Energy, Health & Immigration

Advancing Houston's leadership in energy transition, health equity, and the economic importance of immigration.
Explore →

Houston's future

supporters

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Center for Houston's Future is a nonprofit working to understand future global trends and their impact on the Houston region.
COVID-19 Highlights Challenges for and Advances by Greater Houston’s Health Care Sector

item.title: Our Team
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Our Mission

We aim to ensure Houston remains a great place to live and work for all its residents.

1992
Year founded
0+
Forum graduates
0+
Major initiatives
How We Work

Three key pillars.

We bring business, government, community and academic stakeholders to engage in planning, research, consensus building and action.

Strategic Initiatives

We conduct economic research and strategic planning. Our work currently includes Project Metis, focused on positioning the Houston/Galveston region as the global leader in brain health and the emerging brain economy.

Explore initiatives →

Community Outreach

We hold community events and develop thought leadership. Past strategic initiatives have addressed energy, climate and the energy transition; health and health equity; and the economic importance of immigration.

See events →

Leadership Forum

Our Business/Civic Leadership Forum develops leaders and has graduated over 1,400 alumni, deepening the bench and widening the circle of civic leadership in the region.

Leadership Forum →

Much of our strategic work starts with an economic lens and efforts may include road map and project creation.

Our Story

From partnership to independence.

The Center, also known as CHF, was created in 1992 as an affiliate of the Greater Houston Partnership to develop research projects and educational programs.

In 2000, after input from business and civic leaders, the Center was spun off from the Greater Houston Partnership, as an independent affiliate with an independent board, to focus on deepening the bench and widening the circle of civic leadership in the region.

The Center continues its independent affiliation with the Partnership as well as our Business/Civic Leadership Forum, which has graduated over 1,400 leaders.

Leadership

Our Staff

The professionals leading CHF's strategic initiatives, community engagement, and leadership programs.

item.title: Our Supporters
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Center for Houston's Future is grateful to the generous individuals, foundations, and corporations that support our mission. Their investment enables us to convene cross-sector leaders and drive data-informed policy solutions for the greater Houston region.

Our Generous Supporters

We recognize and thank the following supporters for their commitment to Houston's future.

Founding Partners

Supporter listings managed via Elementor template.

Corporate Sponsors

Sponsor listings managed via Elementor template.

Individual Donors

Donor listings managed via Elementor template.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Center for Houston's Future is supported by leading corporations, foundations, and civic organizations committed to the long-term success of Greater Houston.
Individuals, foundations, and corporations investing in Houston's future.

These corporations, foundations, and civic organizations provide the resources and partnership that make our work possible.

Get in Touch

item.title: News & Media
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Upcoming Events

What's next

View
May 15 2026

CHF Spring Policy Forum

9:00 AM – 12:00 PM Partnership Tower, 701 Avenida de las Americas, Suite 900, Houston

A half-day forum exploring policy priorities for Houston's economic future, featuring panels on energy transition, workforce development, and healthcare innovation.
Jun 10 2026

Project Metis: Brain Health Summit

8:30 AM – 4:00 PM Texas Medical Center, John P. McGovern Campus, Houston

A full-day summit convening leading researchers, healthcare executives, and policymakers to advance Houston's brain health initiative and discuss breakthrough neuroscience research.
Jul 22 2026

Houston Hydrogen Economy Workshop

1:00 – 5:00 PM C. Baldwin, Curio Collection by Hilton, 400 Dallas Street, Houston

Interactive workshop on Houston's role in the emerging hydrogen economy, featuring presentations from HyVelocity Hub partners and industry leaders.
Past Events

Recent gatherings

View
Jan 29 2026

2026 Annual Meeting

5:30 – 7:30 PM Partnership Tower, 701 Avenida de las Americas, Suite 900, Houston

CEO David Gow presents the research and conclusions from Vision 2050, shares how Project Metis is taking shape, and unveils our strategic priorities for the year ahead.
Nov 13 2025

2025 Dinner and Conversation

6:00 – 9:00 PM River Oaks Country Club, TX

Featured discussion: Unleashing the Power of Our Brains — with speakers from UTMB, Rice University, and other institutions.
Oct 21-23 2025

Energy Independence Summit

C. Baldwin, Curio Collection by Hilton, 400 Dallas Street, Houston

Inaugural summit bringing together leaders from energy, finance, and policy to explore American energy independence.
Aug 14 2025

Houston Innovation Summit

9:00 AM – 4:00 PM Marriott Marquis Houston

A full-day summit spotlighting breakthrough innovation across Houston's key sectors, from biotech to aerospace, with networking and demo sessions.
Jun 19 2025

Healthcare Workforce Pipeline Forum

1:00 – 5:00 PM Texas Medical Center

Convening healthcare systems, educators, and workforce development organizations to address critical staffing gaps and build sustainable talent pipelines.
Apr 8 2025

Vision 2050 Community Town Hall

6:00 – 8:00 PM George R. Brown Convention Center

An open community forum presenting early findings from the Vision 2050 study and gathering public input on long-range goals for the Houston region.
Feb 20 2025

Energy Transition Policy Roundtable

8:30 AM – 12:00 PM Partnership Tower

An invitation-only roundtable bringing together energy executives and policy experts to discuss regulatory frameworks supporting Houston's energy transition.
Dec 11 2024

Year-End Leadership Reception

5:30 – 7:30 PM River Oaks Country Club

An evening reception celebrating 2024 accomplishments and previewing the Center's strategic direction for the year ahead with key supporters and community leaders.
Oct 3 2024

Immigration & Workforce Summit

9:00 AM – 3:00 PM C. Baldwin Hotel

A cross-sector summit examining immigration's role in Houston's workforce, featuring panels on policy reform, employer needs, and community integration strategies.
You've seen all past events

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Support the Center for Houston's Future. Your generous contribution helps power research, community programs, and civic leadership for the Houston region.

item.title: Contact Us
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Your support powers our work

The Center for Houston's Future brings business, government, community and academic stakeholders together for planning, research, consensus building and action on issues critical to the Houston region's long-term success. Your generous contribution helps us continue this vital work.

Donate Today

What your donation supports

Every contribution strengthens our ability to drive meaningful change across the Houston region.

Research & Reports

Fund groundbreaking economic research and data-driven insights that inform policy and shape Houston's strategic direction for decades to come.

Community Programs

Support educational initiatives like Driving the Future and WonderWeek that engage young Houstonians and build pathways to opportunity.

Civic Leadership

Develop the next generation of Houston's business and civic leaders through our renowned leadership programs and community forums.

Other Ways to Help

For questions about donating or sponsorship opportunities, contact us at info@futurehouston.org or call (713) 844-9325.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Get in touch with the Center for Houston's Future. Visit our office, call, email, or send us a message.
We'd love to hear from you.

Drop us a line

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Business/Civic Leadership Forum has engaged more than 1,400 diverse leaders from across the Greater Houston region through an immersive civic leadership program.
We inspire leaders to identify matters of the highest importance to the long-term future of the greater Houston region.

item.title: Forum Portal
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Forum

Network. Learn. Engage.

Since 2000, the Business/Civic Leadership Forum has engaged more than 1,400 diverse leaders from across the Greater Houston region through an immersive three-month civic leadership program. Program components include expert speakers, facilitated discussions, and experiential Learning Journeys. Participants examine critical social, economic, and environmental challenges affecting the region.

Forum Application Nominate A Participant Fall 2025 Report
Program

Building passionate civic leaders

Our twice-yearly Business/Civic Leadership Forums build passionate civic leaders as diverse as the Houston region itself, leveraging participants' business and leadership skills to drive lasting change. Programs serve both established and emerging leaders seeking deeper community understanding and civic responsibility.

Schedule

Spring 2026 Forum

March 24
Welcome Reception
5:30 – 7:30 PM
March 31
Galveston Overnight Retreat
5:30 – 9:00 PM
April 1
Galveston Retreat
8:00 AM – 3:00 PM
April 8
Session
1:00 – 5:00 PM
April 22
Session
1:00 – 5:00 PM
May 6
Session
1:00 – 5:00 PM
May 13
Session
5:30 – 7:30 PM
May 20
Session
1:00 – 5:00 PM
June 3
Closing
12:00 – 4:00 PM
0+
leaders engaged
0+
years of programming
2x
forums per year

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Access resources, session materials, and connect with fellow Business/Civic Leadership Forum participants through the CHF Forum Portal.
Access resources, session materials, and connect with fellow forum participants.

item.title: Top 25 Alumni
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Access

Portal Access

Sign in with your forum credentials to access session materials, the participant directory, and exclusive resources.

Forgot password? Not a forum member? Contact us
Resources

Portal Resources

Session Materials

Access presentations, reading materials, and resources from each forum session, available for download and review.

Participant Directory

Browse the current forum cohort and connect with fellow participants to build lasting professional relationships.

Discussion Forum

Engage in facilitated discussions, share insights, and continue the conversation between in-person sessions.

Event Calendar

View upcoming forum sessions, networking events, special speakers, and important deadlines at a glance.

For forum participants only. Contact info@futurehouston.org to request access.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Celebrating outstanding Business/Civic Leadership Forum alumni who exemplify civic leadership and community impact in the Greater Houston region.
Celebrating outstanding alumni who exemplify civic leadership and community impact.

item.title: 75 Leaders Who Stand Apart
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Recognition

Honoring Exceptional Civic Leaders

Each year, the Center for Houston's Future recognizes 25 outstanding alumni of the Business/Civic Leadership Forum who have demonstrated extraordinary commitment to the Houston region. These individuals leverage their forum experience to drive meaningful change across sectors, embodying the spirit of engaged civic leadership that defines our community.

The Top 25 program celebrates alumni who go beyond professional achievement to make lasting contributions to Houston's civic fabric, inspiring the next generation of community leaders.

Criteria

Selection Criteria

1

Community Impact

Demonstrated measurable, positive impact on the Greater Houston region through civic involvement, volunteer service, or community-focused initiatives.

2

Leadership Excellence

Exhibited outstanding leadership qualities that inspire others to engage in civic life, whether through formal positions or grassroots efforts.

3

Cross-sector Collaboration

Built meaningful bridges between business, government, nonprofit, and academic sectors to address complex regional challenges.

4

Sustained Engagement

Maintained ongoing commitment to civic leadership beyond the forum program, continuing to contribute to Houston's long-term prosperity.

Honorees

Past Honorees

2025
View Honorees →
2024
View Honorees →
2023
View Honorees →
2022
View Honorees →

Know an outstanding alumnus? Nominate them for the next Top 25.

Submit a Nomination

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: A special recognition celebrating Houston's most impactful civic leaders through the Center for Houston's Future's 75 Leaders Who Stand Apart program.
A special recognition celebrating Houston's most impactful civic leaders.

item.title: Alumni Directory
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Special Recognition

A Milestone Celebration

To mark a special anniversary in the history of the Center for Houston's Future and the Business/Civic Leadership Forum, we identified 75 extraordinary leaders whose contributions to the Greater Houston region set them apart. These individuals represent the very best of what civic leadership can achieve when vision, dedication, and collaboration come together.

Selected from more than 1,400 forum alumni spanning over two decades, the 75 Leaders Who Stand Apart embody the transformative impact that engaged citizens can have on their community. Their work spans every sector and touches every corner of the Houston region.

75
Leaders Honored
25+
Years of Impact
All
Sectors Represented
About

About This Recognition

The 75 Leaders Who Stand Apart were selected through a rigorous process that considered the depth and breadth of each nominee's civic contributions. A committee of past honorees, board members, and community stakeholders reviewed nominations based on demonstrated leadership, cross-sector collaboration, and sustained commitment to Houston's long-term prosperity.

Honorees were recognized at a special anniversary celebration that brought together past and present forum participants, community leaders, and elected officials. The event highlighted the enduring legacy of the Business/Civic Leadership Forum and its role in shaping Houston's civic landscape for more than two decades.

View the complete list of 75 Leaders →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Connect with more than 1,400 Business/Civic Leadership Forum alumni through the CHF Alumni Directory.
Connect with more than 1,400 Business/Civic Leadership Forum alumni.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Center for Houston's Future strategic initiatives: planning, research and action to position the Houston region for long-term success.
We bring business, government, community and academic stakeholders to engage in planning, research, consensus building and action.

item.title: Privacy Policy
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Current Initiative

Project Metis

The Center is focusing on a bold initiative to position the Houston/Galveston region as the global leader in brain health and the emerging brain economy.

Learn More →
Current Initiative

Vision 2050

The Center, working with business, community, and government leaders, is creating a long-range strategic plan and vision for the Houston region.

Learn More →
Past Work

Initiatives, research & events.

The Center's past initiatives include advancing Houston's leadership in the energy transition, improving health and health equity in our communities, and highlighting the vital economic contributions of immigrants to our region's success.

Immigration → Energy and Climate → Health and Health Equity →
Projects & Reports
HyVelocity Hub → Driving the Future Educational Programming → Houston's Economic Future: Health Care 2022 → Kids in Charge Wonderweek → Social Determinants of Health Impacting Maternal Health → Houston's Economic Future: Immigration →
Conferences & Webinars
Future of Global Energy Conference → Energy and Climate Thought Leadership Webcast Series → How to Have Difficult Conversations on Immigration → COVID-19 Essential Workers and Immigration → Immigration and the Construction Industry →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Privacy Policy for the Center for Houston's Future website. Learn how we collect, use, and protect your information.
Center for Houston's Future Website Privacy Policy

item.title: Driving the Future
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Introduction

Center for Houston's Future values its users' privacy and has designed this policy to assure those affiliated with Center for Houston's Future of our commitment to protecting your information.

Information We Collect

We gather two distinct types of information: (1) Voluntarily provided details including name, address, email address, billing and/or credit card information (for payment processing only); and (2) Automatically collected data through cookies and server logs to assist us in maintaining superior quality service. The Alumni Directory permits logged-in members to view and edit additional information such as Alumni Class, Company Name, Involvement in Social Networks, Areas of Expertise, etc.

Use of Information

Center for Houston's Future does not now, nor will it in the future, sell, rent, or lease any user lists and/or names to any third parties. The organization uses third-party social media platforms including LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Facebook, YouTube, and other interactive programs.

Data Access

Users can request an update, change or removal of your information from our website at any time by contacting the organization at (713) 844-9303 or info@futurehouston.org.

Children's Privacy

We never collect or maintain information on our website from those we know are under 13.

Data Security

We have implemented appropriate physical, electronic, and managerial procedures to safeguard and secure the information we collect online.

Contact

If you have questions about this privacy policy, contact us at info@futurehouston.org or write to:

Center for Houston's Future
701 Avenida de las Americas, Suite 900
Houston, Texas 77010

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: After-school enrichment program about low-carbon energy for elementary and middle school students, in collaboration with Children's Museum Houston.
After-school enrichment program about low-carbon energy

item.title: Kid Charged WonderWeek
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Program Overview

Inspiring tomorrow's energy innovators

The Driving the Future after-school enrichment program focuses on explaining the concepts of a low-carbon future to elementary and middle school students. It includes teacher training, curriculum, classroom supplies, and on-site support, in collaboration with Children's Museum Houston and Houston-area school districts.

The program introduces late elementary and early middle school students to the concept of the energy transition and alternative fuels with the aim of inspiring and empowering them to make a difference in their communities. With concrete and hands-on explorations, our six-week after school program helps prepare students to become innovators of our energy future.

Objectives

Program Goals

1

Increasing students' understanding about the relationship between transportation and climate change

2

Sharing viable options for climate-friendly approaches to transportation

3

Inspiring students to learn more about Houston's leadership in clean fuel technologies

4

Building students' understandings specific to hydrogen as a fuel source

5

Getting students to articulate their personal positions on the future of clean energy

Growth

Expansion Timeline

Spring 2022
Pilot Launch

Pilot sessions with strong evaluation results demonstrated the program's effectiveness in engaging students with low-carbon energy concepts.

Funded by Mitsubishi Corporation
Fall 2022
HISD Expansion

Expanded to 6 additional HISD campuses, bringing the program to hundreds more students across the Houston Independent School District.

CenterPoint Energy Foundation
2023
Galena Park ISD

Extended to Galena Park ISD, further broadening the program's reach to additional communities in the Houston region.

← Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Exploring how innovation goes hand in hand with play, curiosity and experimentation as children discover fuel's future.
Exploring how innovation goes hand in hand with play, curiosity and experimentation

item.title: Signature Conferences
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

Innovation through play

The Center for Houston's Future partnered with Children's Museum Houston to create programming exploring how innovation goes hand in hand with play, curiosity and experimentation as children discover fuel's future.

Kids tested remote-controlled toy cars running on hydrogen fuel and built water molecules to learn how fuel cells change those molecules.

In 2023, Chevron sponsored this event. A larger initiative called 'Driving the Future' was funded by CenterPoint Energy Foundation in 2023. Nearly 150 students across 10 schools participated in a six-session after-school program learning about various energy forms, including low or no-carbon sources. The curriculum was developed by both organizations for grades 3-8.

Program Highlights

Key highlights

Hands-On Learning

Kids tested hydrogen-powered remote-controlled cars, experiencing first-hand how clean fuel can power the vehicles of the future through interactive play.

Science Discovery

Students built water molecules to understand how fuel cells work, transforming abstract chemistry concepts into tangible, hands-on learning experiences.

150 Students

Across 10 schools, nearly 150 students participated in the six-session after-school program learning about various energy forms including low or no-carbon sources.

Collaborators

Partners

Chevron
CenterPoint Energy Foundation
Children's Museum Houston
← Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Center began hosting conferences on low-carbon energy in 2019, co-hosting the Future of Global Energy Conference with the Greater Houston Partnership.
The Center began hosting conferences on low-carbon energy in 2019

item.title: Energy Webcast Series
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

Conferences on low-carbon energy

The Center began hosting conferences on low-carbon energy in 2019. In 2021 and 2022, we co-hosted the Future of Global Energy Conference with the Greater Houston Partnership and programmed panels and webcasts focusing on the role of hydrogen in the energy transition.

2023

2023 Future of Global Energy Conference

Houston's Role in Leading the Energy Transition — Presented by Chevron

1

Community Engagement and Equity

Sponsored by Sempra Infrastructure

2

Funding the Transition

3

Building a Workforce for the Transition

Sponsored by PwC

4

Innovation & Technology for the Transition

5

The Future of Global Energy Luncheon

2022

2022 Virtual Sessions on Hydrogen

1

Hydrogen Hubs

2

Houston Hydrogen Hub Update

3

Next Generation of Hydrogen Technology Development

2021

2021 Virtual Sessions on Hydrogen

1

What Will it Take to Launch Houston's Hydrogen Hub?

2

Who Wins in Producing Hydrogen's Many Colored Rainbow?

3

Growing Early Applications for Low-Carbon Hydrogen in Houston

4

What Federal and State Policies Are Needed? (Session 1)

5

What Federal and State Policies Are Needed? (Session 2)

6

Day 3 Program Summary

← Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Featuring industry leaders, academic experts, journalists and activists on climate and energy transition topics.
Featuring industry leaders, academic experts, journalists and activists on climate and energy transition

item.title: Project Metis
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
About the Series

Energy & Climate Webcast Series

Since 2021, the Center has presented webcasts featuring prominent speakers addressing climate and energy transition topics. Speakers include industry leaders, academic experts, journalists and activists discussing sustainability, ESG, environmental justice, and breakthrough technologies.

Episodes

Webcast Episodes

EP 6

Panel Discussion

Jill Cetina, VP Banking Supervision, Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas • Victor Flatt, Professor of Law, University of Houston • Amanda Hsieh, VP ESG, ClimeCo • Gavin Dillingham, HARC VP (Moderator)

A wide-ranging panel discussion on the intersection of climate policy, financial regulation, environmental law, and corporate ESG strategy, moderated by HARC Vice President Gavin Dillingham.

EP 5

Confronting Climate Gridlock

Daniel Cohan • Raj Mankad

Discussing Confronting Climate Gridlock: How Diplomacy, Technology, and Policy Can Unlock a Clean Energy Future -- exploring the political, technological, and diplomatic pathways toward decarbonization and the obstacles that stand in the way.

EP 4

Saving Us: A Climate Scientist's Case for Hope and Healing in a Divided World

Katharine Hayhoe, Climate Scientist, Texas Tech University & Chief Scientist, The Nature Conservancy • Lisa Gonzalez

Renowned climate scientist Katharine Hayhoe discusses her book Saving Us: A Climate Scientist's Case for Hope and Healing in a Divided World, exploring how meaningful conversations about climate change can bridge divides and inspire collective action.

EP 3

Energy's Digital Future

Amy Myers Jaffe • Raj Mankad

A conversation on Energy's Digital Future: Harnessing Innovation for American Resilience and National Security, examining how digital technologies and innovation can reshape the energy landscape while strengthening national security.

"If the U.S. doesn't lead on the energy transition, China will."
EP 2

Power after Carbon: Building a Clean, Resilient Grid

Peter Fox-Penner • Gavin Dillingham

Discussing Power after Carbon: Building a Clean, Resilient Grid -- how the electric grid can achieve net-zero emissions while maintaining the reliability and affordability that communities and businesses depend on.

EP 1

Reimagining Capitalism in a World on Fire

Rebecca Henderson, Harvard Business School Professor • Bill Langin

Harvard Business School Professor Rebecca Henderson discusses her acclaimed book Reimagining Capitalism in a World on Fire, winner of the Financial Times/McKinsey Book of the Year award, exploring how businesses can drive solutions to climate change and inequality.

← Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Project Metis: A holistic initiative focused on life-long brain health to enhance human flourishing and spur economic growth in the Houston region.
A holistic initiative focused on life-long brain health to enhance human flourishing and spur economic growth.

item.title: Vision 2050
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
The Vision

Positioning Houston as the global leader in brain health

Brain health is the defining frontier of this century. Named after the Greek goddess of wisdom and deep thought, Project Metis reflects our commitment to advancing understanding, prevention, and treatment across the full life cycle of the brain—from early development to healthy aging.

Working with powerhouse medical, academic, and business institutions, we are establishing Houston/Galveston as the epicenter of the emerging brain economy—a new era where brain health and cognitive skills serve as key levers for economic growth and human potential.

Read the Press Release →
Our Focus Areas

Five interconnected fronts

Early Life

Supporting optimal brain development from prenatal through childhood years.

Workplace Years

Promoting cognitive health, mental wellness, and peak performance in the working population.

Healthy Aging

Advancing prevention, early detection, and care for age-related cognitive conditions.

Medical/Science

Accelerating research breakthroughs in neuroscience, treatment, and recovery.

Innovation & Commercialization

Building an ecosystem to recruit, incubate, and scale brain health startups and technologies.

Our Approach

The next 18 months

1

Convene and Collaborate

Launch expert working teams focused on brain health across all life stages, scientific advances, and innovation.

2

Measure Progress

Develop a regional Brain Health Index to track outcomes and equity.

3

Pilot and Scale

Implement breakthrough projects in clinical care, education, workplace wellness, and community health.

4

Build the Ecosystem

Recruit and support neuro-startups, connect researchers with entrepreneurs, and foster commercialization.

5

Lead Globally

Share Houston's progress and learnings at major international forums, including Davos and the UN General Assembly.

6

Inspire Action

Host high-visibility events and thought leadership to engage the community and drive momentum.

Partners & Supporters

Steering Committee

JR
Founding Chair
Dr. Jochen Reiser, CEO, MD, PhD
President of UTMB and CEO of the UTMB Health System
AD
Project Chair
Amy Dittmar
EVP and Provost, Rice University
DC
Project Chair
Dr. David L. Callender
CEO, Memorial Hermann Health System
Inaugural Steering Committee Members
bp
bp
Global Energy Company
EM
Eric Mullins
Steering Committee Member
MD
UT MD Anderson Cancer Center
The University of Texas

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Vision 2050: Creating a long-range strategic blueprint and vision for the Houston region, working with business, community, and government leaders.
Creating a long-range strategic blueprint and vision for the Houston region.

item.title: Energy and Climate
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
The Vision

A thriving region for all

Working with business, community, and government leaders, we are creating a long-range strategic framework for the Houston region that looks out 5 to 25 years. We will share an aspirational vision and blueprint for a thriving region for all, which will highlight great work already underway and detail next-generation solutions for critical focus areas.

Support Vision 2050 →
Our Process

From data to action

1

Research & Input

We collected data and integrated input from diverse voices across our region.

2

Expert Deep Dives

Expert working teams conducted deep dives into each pillar, creating visionary frameworks with creative, next-generation solutions.

3

Compelling Story

These comprehensive plans were combined into one compelling multimedia story, presented at the 2026 Annual Meeting.

Together, we're building a thriving Houston region for generations to come.
View Final Vision 2050 Overview →
Strategic Pillars

Five pillars for Houston's future

Economic Vitality

Building a diversified, resilient economy

Livability & Infrastructure

Creating connected, sustainable communities

Health & Well-Being

Ensuring equitable health outcomes for all

Education & Workforce

Preparing talent for tomorrow's economy

Civic Engagement

Strengthening inclusive governance and participation

Founding Partners

Supported by visionary leaders

Founding Partners
NRG / Reliant
The George Foundation
Founding Supporters
Marcia Faschingbauer Bill Boyar Judson Robinson III Roland Garcia Ann Lents David Abraham Astley Blair Amy Pierce Steve Kean

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Center works to ensure that the Houston region is a global leader in the energy transition.
The Center works to ensure that the Houston region is a global leader in the energy transition.

item.title: Health & Health Equity
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

Leading the energy transition

For the past six years, the Center has led efforts to establish Houston as a leader in the energy transition in setting a path to reduce emissions and provide low-carbon services to the region and the world. Our efforts have focused on groundbreaking economic research, coalition building, vision setting and action, thought leadership and raising awareness around climate and the energy transition.

Key Research

Groundbreaking reports

Featured

HyVelocity Hub

Comprised of leading energy companies and organizations, HyVelocity Hub applied for Department of Energy Regional Clean Hydrogen Hub funding to accelerate the development of clean hydrogen projects in Texas, Southwest Louisiana and the U.S. Gulf Coast.

Read More →
Report

Houston's Future as a Global Center for Clean Hydrogen Manufacturing, Recycling and Electrolysis

The Center has conducted extensive research on low-carbon energy and the energy transition, including around the development of a clean hydrogen hub in Houston and economic analysis on the impacts of the energy industry.

Read the Report →
Report

Houston as the Epicenter of a Global Clean Hydrogen Hub

With the U.S. Department of Energy announcing plans for $8 billion in funding for clean hydrogen hubs across the country, our new report finds that Houston is well positioned to become a global clean hydrogen hub.

Read the Report →
Work & Events

Projects & conferences

Projects
Educational Programming → HyVelocity Hub → Kid Charged Wonderweek → HETI →
Conferences & Presentations
Signature Conferences and Presentations → Energy and Climate Thought Leadership Webcast Series → Houston as the Epicenter of a Global Hydrogen Hub → Houston's Future as a Global Center for Clean Hydrogen Manufacturing → Pathways to Decarbonize the PVC Value Chain in 2050 →
In the News

Latest updates

Feb 13, 2026 Energy Jobs: a guiding document for Houston's Workforce → Dec 9, 2025 Powerhouse Institutions across Houston/Galveston Launch Project Metis → Sep 25, 2025 Center for Houston's Future launches Brain Initiative →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Learn about CHF's Health and Health Equity initiative, addressing healthcare challenges and promoting equitable health outcomes across the Houston region.
The Center is committed to research and collaborations to improve health and health equity in our communities.

item.title: Immigration
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
OVERVIEW

A paradox of plenty

In 2019, the Center began research on the role of health care and health in Houston's economy. We conducted roundtable discussions, workshops, surveys, and interviews with experts. The result, a 2020 research paper, featured a range of findings, including that Houston has a ‘paradox of plenty’ with world-class health care institutions alongside some of nation's worst health outcomes; and that Houston is poised to become a leader in life science technology innovations.

OUR RESEARCH

Published reports

Houston's Future: AI in Healthcare

Artificial intelligence is reshaping Houston's healthcare landscape. Our 2024 report explores how the city's renowned medical institutions and diverse population position it to lead in AI-driven innovations. We examine current applications in diagnostics, personalized treatments, and patient care optimization, while addressing critical challenges like ensuring algorithmic fairness.

Read the Report

Houston's Future: Climate Change and Health Care

In 2024, we explore the critical intersection of climate change and health care in this report that highlights escalating health threats, from heatstroke to mosquito-borne illness, that our region faces due to climate change and includes a series of recommendations to address these challenges.

Read the Report

Houston's Economic Future: Health Care, 2022 Report

In 2022, we released an update to our 2020 report looking at regional trends in the wake of COVID-19. The report's economic modeling suggests developing the region's life sciences industries and health care workforce will yield major long-term benefits.

Read the Report

Social Determinants of Health Impacting Maternal Health

In 2021, we focused on the state of maternal health as a key indicator of any health system and looked at non-medical drivers of health, which are estimated to drive 70% of outcomes.

Read the Report

Houston's Economic Future: Health Care, 2020 Report

The Center began research on the role of health care and health in Houston's economy in 2019. The result featured a range of findings, including that Houston has a ‘paradox of plenty’ with world-class health care institutions alongside some of the nation's worst health outcomes.

Read the Report
PRESENTATIONS

Conferences and media

2022 Report Release Presentation

HCBH Conference Presentation

ExamiNATION Episode 9: Supporting the Workforce

ExamiNATION Episode 10: Where We Go From Here

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Center focuses on the long-term economic importance of immigrants and immigration and their significant contributions to the region's success.
The Center focuses on the long-term economic importance of immigrants and immigration and their significant contributions to the region's success.

item.title: Climate Change & Health Care
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

Economic importance of immigration

The Center began working on immigration in 2017, focusing on the economic importance of immigrants and immigration to the success of the Greater Houston region. Since launching our Strategic Initiative on immigration, we have created an economic model, published two groundbreaking reports, convened focus groups and hosted a number of public discussions. We continue to engage with a broad group of collaborating organizations to address immigration issues, particularly through the lens of Houston's long-term economic outlook.

Our Research

Groundbreaking reports

Houston's Economic Future: Immigration (2021 Update)

We published a groundbreaking 2019 report and followed with a 2021 update. Each elaborates on how Houston's economic growth is intertwined with its demography. We discuss how immigration has historically fueled the region's economy and why it will be key to our economic future. The findings show curtailing immigration would significantly depress economic growth.

Read the Report →

Houston's Economic Future: Immigration (2019 Study)

Looking ahead to Houston's 200th anniversary in 2036, we developed an economic forecasting tool to analyze how changes in immigration would affect the region's economy. Our findings confirm that curtailing immigration would significantly depress economic growth.

Read the Report →
Partnerships

Collaborative partners and projects

Rational Middle and the Center for the United States and Mexico (CUSMX) at Rice University's Baker Institute have been among our closest collaborators on immigration. The Center worked with these organizations to produce a series of short documentaries to present the facts on immigration and frame them with historical context and personal stories.

In 2020, we hosted a series of webcasts on immigration intended to advance the conversation about immigration and its economic importance in Houston and Texas.

Explore Projects →
Resources
Resources on Immigration → CHF-CUSMX Workshop Report → Rational Middle →
Panel Discussions
How to Have Difficult Conversations on Immigration → COVID-19 Essential Workers and Immigration → Immigration and the Construction Industry →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Exploring the critical intersection of climate change and health care in the Houston region.
Exploring the critical intersection of climate change and health care in the Houston region.

item.title: Health Care 2020
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
OVERVIEW

Climate change and health care in Houston

In 2024, we explore the critical intersection of climate change and health care in this report that highlights escalating health threats, from heatstroke to mosquito-borne illness, that our region faces due to climate change and includes a series of recommendations to address these challenges. Health care leaders are on the front lines of treating these health issues and addressing the sector's contribution to GHG emissions. Collaboration within and among health systems is vital to safeguarding the health of Houston's families against the backdrop of an evolving climate landscape.

KEY THEMES

Critical areas of focus

Escalating Health Threats

From heatstroke to mosquito-borne illness, climate change poses growing risks to Houston's communities.

Health Care's Carbon Footprint

The health care sector must address its own contribution to greenhouse gas emissions.

Collaboration Is Vital

Within and among health systems, collaboration is key to safeguarding community health.

Back to Health and Health Equity

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Explore CHF's Health Care 2020 initiative, a comprehensive study of healthcare access and outcomes in the Greater Houston area.

item.title: Health Care 2022
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
REPORT OVERVIEW

Researching health care's role in Houston's economy

The Center began research on the role of health care and health in Houston's economy in 2019. We conducted roundtable discussions, workshops, surveys, and interviews with experts. The result, a 2020 research paper, featured a range of findings, including that Houston has a 'paradox of plenty' with world-class health care institutions alongside some of the nation's worst health outcomes; and that Houston is poised to become a leader in life science technology innovations.

KEY FINDINGS

What the research revealed

Paradox of Plenty
World-class health care institutions exist alongside some of the nation's worst health outcomes.
Life Science Innovation
Houston is poised to become a leader in life science technology innovations.
Economic Impact
Health care is a major driver of Houston's economy, with significant workforce and GDP implications.
Back to Health and Health Equity

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Discover the findings of CHF's Health Care 2022 report on the state of healthcare in the Houston region.
COVID-19 Highlights Challenges for and Advances by Greater Houston’s Health Care Sector

item.title: Maternal Health
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
REPORT OVERVIEW

Understanding COVID-19's impact on Houston health care

Based on over 50 expert interviews, three roundtable discussions, extensive literature reviews, in-depth data analysis, and proprietary economic modeling, this 2022 report explores how Health and Health Equity affect our region. It examines COVID-19's profound impact on healthcare with both challenges and advancements, highlighting health disparities, pandemic innovations, and workforce challenges.

KEY FINDINGS

By the numbers

1 in 6
jobs added from 2021 to 2036 predicted to be in healthcare
$63.5B
boost to the local economy
11.2%
Houston's health care industry share of employment
23 years
life expectancy variance depending on zip code
$14B
potential GDP cost if workforce issues go unaddressed
15,000
died from COVID-19 in Houston MSA by August 2022
PRESENTATIONS

Conferences and media

2022 Report Release Presentation

HCBH Conference Presentation

ExamiNATION Episode 9: Supporting the Workforce

ExamiNATION Episode 10: Where We Go From Here
Back to Health and Health Equity

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Business-based community engagement can improve maternal health. Explore social determinants of health impacting maternal outcomes in Houston.
Business-based community engagement can improve maternal health, report finds

item.title: AI in Healthcare
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
REPORT OVERVIEW

Social determinants of maternal health

The report explores Social Determinants of Health Impacting Maternal Health and the Opportunities to Improve Outcomes. Despite substantial healthcare spending, the U.S. faces alarmingly high maternal mortality rates. The pandemic has further highlighted and deepened regional disparities, particularly affecting Black women.

KEY FINDINGS

The data behind the crisis

3x

Black mothers are three times as likely to die in childbirth

100%+

Pre-term birth rates in under-resourced Houston zip codes exceed others by more than 100%

50%

Black mothers are 50% more likely to experience pre-term birth than white mothers

70%

Non-medical drivers are estimated to drive 70% of health outcomes

RECOMMENDATIONS

Pathways to better outcomes

1

Family-friendly workplace policies

Internal policy implementation promoting family-friendly workplaces that support maternal health through flexible scheduling, adequate leave, and comprehensive benefits.

2

Employee health education

Employee education on available health services, ensuring workers are aware of and can access prenatal care, mental health support, and postpartum resources.

3

Cooperative community investment

Cooperative community investment to enhance health outcomes through business-led partnerships that address social determinants of health in under-resourced areas.

Back to Health and Health Equity

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Explore how artificial intelligence is transforming healthcare delivery and outcomes in the Houston region through CHF's research and initiatives.
Houston can become a global hub for medical artificial intelligence, new report finds.

item.title: Clean Hydrogen Hub
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

A Landmark Report

The Center for Houston's Future has unveiled a groundbreaking report titled "Houston's Future: AI in Healthcare," showcasing the city's potential to become a global leader in medical artificial intelligence. This comprehensive study, based on extensive research and expert interviews, provides an in-depth analysis of AI's transformative role in Houston's medical ecosystem.

Research

Key Findings

The report identifies four critical areas shaping the future of AI-driven healthcare in Houston.

AI's Impact

AI is already revolutionizing healthcare in Houston, enhancing diagnostics, personalizing treatments, and optimizing patient care.

Houston's Unique Position

World-class medical institutions, academic research, and a diverse population position Houston as a leader in medical AI innovation.

Challenges

Data privacy concerns, integration with existing systems, and the need to address potential biases in AI algorithms present significant hurdles.

Financial & Training Barriers

Financial constraints and the need for comprehensive training among healthcare professionals remain critical barriers to adoption.

Action Plan

Strategic Recommendations

Ten actionable strategies to accelerate responsible AI adoption across Houston's healthcare landscape.

1
Foster Collaboration
2
Establish Robust Data Infrastructure
3
Prioritize Equity
4
Invest in Workforce Development
5
Leverage Small and Big Data
6
Focus on "Raising the Floor"
7
Support AI Innovation
8
Measure and Communicate Impact
9
Engage Policymakers
10
Build Public Trust
“
By leveraging the collective wisdom, resources, and innovation concentrated in Houston's medical community, the city can address the challenges and opportunities presented by AI in healthcare.
David Gow Center for Houston's Future
← Back to Health and Health Equity

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Learn about the Gulf Coast Clean Hydrogen Hub initiative and CHF's role in advancing hydrogen energy infrastructure in the Houston region.
Houston as the Epicenter of a Global Clean Hydrogen Hub

item.title: Hydrogen Manufacturing
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Report Overview

This report provides the most detailed assessment to date of the economic potential and environmental impact of clean hydrogen. Clean hydrogen can be produced through two approaches: adding carbon capture to natural gas-based hydrogen plants or electrolysis (splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen).

The Houston region produces and consumes a third of the nation's hydrogen and has more than 50 percent of the country's dedicated hydrogen pipelines.

Key Stats

1/3
Of the nation's hydrogen produced and consumed in the Houston region
50%+
Of the country's dedicated hydrogen pipelines in the region
$8B
In DOE funding for clean hydrogen hubs nationwide
100+
Experts from 70 companies consulted
70
Organizations across the hydrogen value chain involved

Report Development

The report was created through the Greater Houston Partnership's Houston Energy Transition Initiative, incorporating input from over 100 experts representing 70 companies and organizations across the hydrogen value chain. McKinsey and Company provided research and economic analyses.

Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Explore how hydrogen manufacturing is being developed in Houston to support clean energy transition and economic growth.
Houston’s Future as a Global Center for Clean Hydrogen Manufacturing, Recycling and Electrolysis

item.title: HyVelocity Hub
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Strategic Advantages for a Hydrogen Hub

Houston holds strategic advantages for becoming a global hydrogen energy hub — specifically for manufacturing equipment key to future hydrogen production and distribution infrastructure in the United States and worldwide.

This new economy will provide significant economic and social benefits as Houston becomes a preeminent global hub for the manufacture and deployment of hydrogen large scale electrolyzer facilities. Houston's existing status as a global hub for planning and manufacturing energy systems make it an essential element in any larger scale national plan for transitioning to a hydrogen economy.

Research Methodology

To assess the region's potential as a hub for hydrogen and hydrogen equipment manufacturing, analysis by Partners in Performance and the Center was conducted through interviews and consultation with 100+ market players as well as study of global, national, and local conditions.

Suppliers

Existing businesses with relevance to the supply chain

Integrators

Companies that integrate components for large-scale electrolyzer systems

Enablers

Public authorities, institutions and groups creating supportive conditions

Key Finding

This engagement confirms Houston's intrinsic strengths and growing opportunities for providing the 'picks and shovels' of the green hydrogen gold rush.

100+
Market players interviewed
Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Discover the HyVelocity Hub, a collaborative effort to develop clean hydrogen infrastructure along the Gulf Coast.
A commitment to advance clean, affordable, at-scale hydrogen in Texas and Louisiana

item.title: Immigration Report 2021
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
About the HyVelocity Hub

The HyVelocity Hub is a collaborative comprised of non-profit research organizations, academia, and leading energy companies working to advance the clean hydrogen ecosystem in Texas, Southwest Louisiana, and along the U.S. Gulf Coast.

With more than 1,000 miles of dedicated hydrogen pipelines, and 48 hydrogen production plants, the Gulf Coast is already the nation's largest hydrogen producer. It is home to a diverse array of energy resources, including hydrogen production facilities and pipelines, a large base of industrial energy consumers, and a skilled workforce.

HyVelocity is an industry-led hub that includes seven core industry participants: AES Corporation, Air Liquide, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Mitsubishi Power Americas, Orsted, and Sempra Infrastructure. CHF, GTI Energy and the University of Texas-Austin were founding organizers of HyVelocity.

Visit HyVelocityHub.com

By the Numbers

1,000+
Miles of hydrogen pipelines
48
Hydrogen production plants
7
Core industry participants

Core Industry Partners

Seven leading energy companies driving the hydrogen economy

AES Corporation Air Liquide Chevron ExxonMobil Mitsubishi Power Americas Orsted Sempra Infrastructure
Founding Organizers CHF GTI Energy University of Texas-Austin
Back to Energy and Climate

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: 2021 Report Update demonstrating how decreased immigration rates depressed future economic growth in the Greater Houston region.
2021 Report Update demonstrating how decreased immigration rates depressed future economic growth

item.title: 2019 Immigration Study
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
← Back to Immigration
Report Overview

2021 Immigration Report Update

This update to the 2019 report demonstrated how the recent two-year decrease in the region's immigration rates depressed future economic growth, confirming that economic growth is critically dependent on continued immigration.

Key Findings

What the data reveals

Economic Loss
$47B

Economic loss under restricted immigration scenario (30% less) by 2040

Economic Gain
$62B

Economic gain under increased immigration scenario (30% more) by 2040

Workforce
1 in 3

Immigrants already make up nearly one-third of the region's workforce

Future Growth
2 in 5

Jobs added by 2040 will be filled by foreign-born workers

Declining Trend
25,000 → 16,000

Annual new immigrant workers dropped from the 2007–2016 average to the 2016–2019 average

Sector Analysis

Industries examined through 2040

Professional Services

The report provides sector-specific analysis of professional services through 2040

Manufacturing

The report provides sector-specific analysis of manufacturing through 2040

Energy

The report provides sector-specific analysis of the energy industry through 2040

Information

The report provides sector-specific analysis of the information sector through 2040

Resources
Resources on Immigration → CHF-CUSMX Workshop Report → Rational Middle →
Panel Discussions
How to Have Difficult Conversations on Immigration → COVID-19 Essential Workers and Immigration → Immigration and the Construction Industry →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Houston's Economic Future: Immigration. A 2019 study analyzing how changes in immigration would affect the Greater Houston region's economy through its 200th anniversary in 2036.

item.title: Difficult Conversations on Immigration
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
← Back to Immigration
Overview

Houston's Economic Future & Immigration

Looking ahead to Houston's 200th anniversary in 2036, we developed an economic forecasting tool to analyze how changes in immigration would affect the region's economy. Our findings confirm that curtailing immigration would significantly depress economic growth.

Report Highlights

What the study covers

1

Immigration System Overview

A brief explainer of the current immigration system and how policies have shaped Houston

2

Local Policy Options

An overview of possible local policies that could make the region more welcoming to immigrants

3

Evolving Demographics

A profile of the region's evolving immigrant population, highlighting how Houston's foreign-born residents are becoming higher-skilled and more integrated

4

Industry Perspectives

Summarized findings from focus groups with regional leaders on immigration-related challenges and opportunities

Resources
Resources on Immigration → CHF-CUSMX Workshop Report → Rational Middle →
Panel Discussions
How to Have Difficult Conversations on Immigration → COVID-19 Essential Workers and Immigration → Immigration and the Construction Industry →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: How to Have Difficult Conversations on Immigration. Panel discussion providing ideas for structuring conversations about immigration policy and economic impact.
How to Have Difficult Conversations on Immigration

item.title: COVID-19 Essential Workers & Immigration
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
← Back to Immigration
Overview

Structuring conversations about immigration

Immigration will continue as a major issue with differing perspectives among friends and family. This panel discussion provides ideas for structuring conversations about immigration. Panelists shared useful facts and discussed what to say and how to say it when facts don't resonate.

This panel continues work with Rational Middle, New American Economy, and the Center for the United States and Mexico (CUSMX) at Rice University's Baker Institute, advancing conversation about immigration policy and economic impact.

Panel Discussion

Meet the panelists

LC
Moderator

Laura Collins

Director, George W. Bush Institute

Former Director of Immigration Policy at American Action Forum.

ZG
Panelist

Zaira Garcia

Texas State Director, FWD.US

Advocacy organization focused on immigration and criminal justice reform.

CM
Panelist

Candy Marshall

President, TheDream.US

National scholarship program for Dreamers; former leader at Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.

DV
Panelist

Dan Vallone

Director, More in Common USA

Education policy background; former Army infantry officer.

← Back to Immigration

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: COVID-19 Essential Workers and Immigration panel exploring the role of immigrant workers in essential services during the pandemic.

item.title: Immigration & Construction Industry
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

COVID-19 Essential Workers and Immigration

This panel continued partnership with Rational Middle and the Center for the United States and Mexico (CUSMX) at Rice University's Baker Institute to advance conversation about immigration and its economic importance in Houston and Texas.

Immigrant workers, both documented and undocumented, play an important role in the country's workforce. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted reliance on these workers for essential services. Agriculture and meat packing, food distribution, grocery retail, healthcare, research, and delivery sectors rely significantly on immigrant labor. The panel explores facts about these numbers, worker valuation and safety, and current immigration policy effects on pandemic response.

Panel Discussion

Panelists

DB

David J. Bier

Immigration Policy Analyst, Cato Institute

Associate director of immigration studies at the Cato Institute. His work has been published in the New York Times, Washington Post, and USA Today.

GC

Guadalupe Correa-Cabrera, Ph.D.

Associate Professor, George Mason University

Non-resident Scholar at the Center for the United States and Mexico (CUSMX) at Rice University's Baker Institute. Author of "Los Zetas Inc."

JG

Julia Gelatt, Ph.D.

Senior Policy Analyst, Migration Policy Institute

Ph.D. in sociology (demography) from Princeton University. Senior Policy Analyst at the Migration Policy Institute, focusing on immigration policy research and analysis.

KJ

Ken Janda

Principal, Wild Blue Health Solutions

Adjunct Professor at the University of Houston College of Medicine and Rice University's Jones School of Business. Former CEO of Community Health Choice.

← Back to Immigration

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: The Center hosted a conversation about immigration and the construction industry, featuring the book Deconstructed by Loren Steffy and Stan Marek.

item.title: Immigration Partners & Projects
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Overview

Immigration and the building trades

The Center hosted a conversation with Loren Steffy about his new book with Stan Marek, Deconstructed: An Insider's View of Illegal Immigration and the Building Trades.

Immigrants have long been the source of America's economic strength. Yet our immigration laws have been ill-defined and arbitrary, based largely on the fears of the moment and aimed at limiting particular groups of people.

Deconstructed unravels these economic issues and shows the ineffectiveness of America's policies through the eyes of a man who has watched first-hand the immigration crisis unfold over the past 40 years.

Featured Book

About the book

Deconstructed: An Insider's View of Illegal Immigration and the Building Trades

By Loren Steffy and Stan Marek

An insider account that unravels the complex economic issues surrounding immigration in the construction industry, revealing the ineffectiveness of America's policies through the eyes of a man who has watched first-hand the immigration crisis unfold over the past 40 years in the building trades.

← Back to Immigration

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Collaborative Partners and Projects on immigration including Rational Middle and CUSMX at Rice University's Baker Institute.

item.title: Immigration Resources
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
← Back to Immigration
Overview

Collaborative Partners and Projects

Rational Middle and the Center for the United States and Mexico (CUSMX) at Rice University's Baker Institute have been among our closest collaborators on immigration. The Center worked with these organizations to produce a series of short documentaries presenting immigration facts with historical context and personal narratives. These videos feature perspectives from immigrants, municipalities, researchers, business owners, and citizens addressing immigration challenges.

The Center and CUSMX collaborated on immigration workshops and summits featuring national scholars and experts, promoting research and fact-based analysis supporting economic growth through rational immigration policy.

In 2020, the organization hosted a series of webcasts on immigration designed to advance conversation about immigration's economic importance in Houston and Texas.

Partner Organizations

Featured collaborators

Rational Middle

Documentary series presenting immigration facts with historical context and personal stories.

Visit Rational Middle →

CUSMX at Rice Baker Institute

Immigration workshops and summits featuring national scholars and experts.

Visit Baker Institute →
Resources
Watch Videos from Rational Middle → CHF-CUSMX Workshop Report → Immigration Webcasts →

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Thoughtful, non-partisan research on immigration from our partners and collaborators. Resources from leading policy institutes and research organizations.
Thoughtful, non-partisan research on immigration from our partners and collaborators

item.title: CHF Spring Policy Forum
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Partner Organizations

Research & Policy Resources

Explore immigration research and policy analysis from these leading non-partisan organizations.

Bipartisan Policy Center
Cato Institute
FWD.us
George W. Bush Institute
Migration Policy Institute
National Immigration Forum
New American Economy
Rice University's Baker Institute CUSMX
← Back to Immigration

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Join CHF for a half-day policy forum on Houston's economic future featuring panels on energy, workforce, and healthcare.

item.title: Project Metis: Brain Health Summit
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
9:00 AM – 12:00 PM
Partnership Tower, 701 Avenida de las Americas, Suite 900, Houston
Register Now →

A half-day forum exploring policy priorities for Houston's economic future, featuring panels on energy transition, workforce development, and healthcare innovation.

Panelists include leaders from the energy sector, healthcare institutions, and city government. Topics include Houston's role in the national energy transition, workforce pipeline development, and emerging health technology investments.

This forum builds on CHF's decades of cross-sector convening experience, bringing together diverse stakeholders to shape actionable policy recommendations for the Houston region.

Location

Register for This Event

Secure your spot. All fields are required unless noted.

Or register via Eventbrite

← Back to Events

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Full-day summit advancing Houston's brain health initiative with leading researchers and healthcare executives.

item.title: Houston Hydrogen Economy Workshop
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
8:30 AM – 4:00 PM
Texas Medical Center, John P. McGovern Campus, Houston
Register Now →

A full-day summit convening leading researchers, healthcare executives, and policymakers to advance Houston's brain health initiative and discuss breakthrough neuroscience research.

The summit features keynote addresses from leading neuroscientists, panel discussions on translational research, and interactive workshops on clinical applications. Attendees will have the opportunity to connect with researchers from Rice University, UTMB, and the Texas Medical Center.

Project Metis represents an unprecedented collaboration among Houston's leading institutions, and this summit marks a major milestone in the initiative's development.

Location

Register for This Event

Secure your spot. All fields are required unless noted.

Or register via Eventbrite

← Back to Events

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Interactive workshop on Houston's hydrogen economy with HyVelocity Hub partners and industry leaders.

item.title: 2026 Annual Meeting
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
1:00 – 5:00 PM
C. Baldwin, Curio Collection by Hilton, 400 Dallas Street, Houston
Register Now →

Interactive workshop on Houston's role in the emerging hydrogen economy, featuring presentations from HyVelocity Hub partners and industry leaders.

Topics include hydrogen production technologies, infrastructure development along the Gulf Coast, workforce training programs, and federal funding opportunities.

This workshop builds on CHF's extensive research into clean hydrogen's potential to drive economic growth in the Houston region, leveraging the city's existing energy infrastructure and workforce expertise.

Location

Register for This Event

Secure your spot. All fields are required unless noted.

Or register via Eventbrite

← Back to Events

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: CHF's 2026 Annual Meeting featuring Vision 2050 findings and Project Metis updates.

item.title: 2025 Dinner and Conversation
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
5:30 – 7:30 PM
Partnership Tower, 701 Avenida de las Americas, Suite 900, Houston

CEO David Gow presents the research and conclusions from Vision 2050, shares how Project Metis is taking shape, and unveils our strategic priorities for the year ahead.

The annual meeting brought together over 200 business, civic, and academic leaders for an evening of strategic discussion about Houston's future. Highlights included the public preview of Vision 2050 findings and the announcement of new partnerships under the Project Metis initiative.

Attendees engaged in lively Q&A sessions and networking, reflecting the collaborative spirit that defines CHF's approach to regional planning.

Event Gallery

Location

← Back to Events

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: CHF's 2025 Dinner and Conversation on brain health research with speakers from UTMB and Rice.

item.title: Energy Independence Summit
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
6:00 – 9:00 PM
River Oaks Country Club, TX

Featured discussion: Unleashing the Power of Our Brains — with speakers from UTMB, Rice University, and other institutions.

This signature event brought together Houston's top leaders for an intimate dinner and moderated conversation about the emerging field of brain health research. Speakers discussed how Houston's unique concentration of medical institutions positions the city as a potential global leader in neuroscience.

The evening sparked conversations that ultimately contributed to the formation of Project Metis, CHF's groundbreaking brain health initiative.

Event Gallery

Location

← Back to Events

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Three-day summit exploring American energy independence with leaders from energy, finance, and policy.

item.title: About
item.dc:creator: admin
item.content:encoded:
Multi-day event (October 21–23)
C. Baldwin, Curio Collection by Hilton, 400 Dallas Street, Houston

Inaugural summit bringing together leaders from energy, finance, and policy to explore American energy independence.

Over three days, participants engaged in panel discussions, networking sessions, and workshops covering topics from clean hydrogen production to carbon capture technology. The summit positioned Houston as the natural hub for America's energy future, leveraging the city's existing infrastructure and expertise.

Key themes included the role of natural gas as a transition fuel, investment trends in clean energy, and policy frameworks needed to accelerate the energy transition while maintaining economic competitiveness.

Event Gallery

Location

← Back to Events
